message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Mechanical projection over a bash-authored group; mirrors the colophon/rbcc shape against the fixture-name owner. Carries a premise correction from the prior docket.
  
  ## Goal
  
  Project the bash-authored fixture names into generated `RBTDGC_*` consts via the shared `buz_emit_const` primitive, and repoint their consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.
  
  ## Shape
  
  - Fixture names live spread across the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays in `rbte_engine.sh` — there is no single canonical array today. A minimal tidy is required: declare the fixture set once as a flat emit list; the suite arrays stay literal subsets (they select fixtures, they do not declare them). Suite-vs-list drift is already caught — an unknown fixture name fails resolution.
  - Only the bash-authored fixtures project (those named in the suite arrays). The calibrant fixtures are Rust-only synthetic (`rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`, no bash home) and stay out of scope per the heat's single-bash-author rule — their `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_*` consts remain hand-written in the manifest. The hand-mirror shrinks; it does not vanish.
  - Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem, hyphens to underscores, uppercased); migrate the bash-authored manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts. Mirror `rbcc_emit_consts` for the emit shape and `rbz_emit_consts` for the combiner wiring.
  - Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.
  
  ## Sources
  
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name author (suite arrays)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` — Rust-only fixtures (out of scope)
  
  ## What done looks like
  
  The bash-authored fixture consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; their consumers reference the generated names; the calibrant consts remain in the manifest; Rust builds deny-warnings clean and the fast suite is green.

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BP
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.
6	
7	The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.
8	
9	## Locked decisions
10	
11	- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
12	- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
13	- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
14	- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.
15	
16	## What done looks like
17	
18	The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).
19	
20	# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAG
21	
22	## Character
23	
24	Mechanical projection over a bash-authored group; mirrors the colophon/rbcc shape against the fixture-name owner. Carries a premise correction from the prior docket.
25	
26	## Goal
27	
28	Project the bash-authored fixture names into generated `RBTDGC_*` consts via the shared `buz_emit_const` primitive, and repoint their consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.
29	
30	## Shape
31	
32	- Fixture names live spread across the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays in `rbte_engine.sh` — there is no single canonical array today. A minimal tidy is required: declare the fixture set once as a flat emit list; the suite arrays stay literal subsets (they select fixtures, they do not declare them). Suite-vs-list drift is already caught — an unknown fixture name fails resolution.
33	- Only the bash-authored fixtures project (those named in the suite arrays). The calibrant fixtures are Rust-only synthetic (`rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`, no bash home) and stay out of scope per the heat's single-bash-author rule — their `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_*` consts remain hand-written in the manifest. The hand-mirror shrinks; it does not vanish.
34	- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem, hyphens to underscores, uppercased); migrate the bash-authored manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts. Mirror `rbcc_emit_consts` for the emit shape and `rbz_emit_consts` for the combiner wiring.
35	- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.
36	
37	## Sources
38	
39	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name author (suite arrays)
40	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror
41	- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` — Rust-only fixtures (out of scope)
42	
43	## What done looks like
44	
45	The bash-authored fixture consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; their consumers reference the generated names; the calibrant consts remain in the manifest; Rust builds deny-warnings clean and the fast suite is green.
46

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAG

## Character

Mechanical projection over a bash-authored group; mirrors the colophon/rbcc shape against the fixture-name owner. Carries a premise correction from the prior docket.

## Goal

Project the bash-authored fixture names into generated `RBTDGC_*` consts via the shared `buz_emit_const` primitive, and repoint their consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Fixture names live spread across the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays in `rbte_engine.sh` — there is no single canonical array today. A minimal tidy is required: declare the fixture set once as a flat emit list; the suite arrays stay literal subsets (they select fixtures, they do not declare them). Suite-vs-list drift is already caught — an unknown fixture name fails resolution.
- Only the bash-authored fixtures project (those named in the suite arrays). The calibrant fixtures are Rust-only synthetic (`rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`, no bash home) and stay out of scope per the heat's single-bash-author rule — their `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_*` consts remain hand-written in the manifest. The hand-mirror shrinks; it does not vanish.
- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem, hyphens to underscores, uppercased); migrate the bash-authored manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts. Mirror `rbcc_emit_consts` for the emit shape and `rbz_emit_consts` for the combiner wiring.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name author (suite arrays)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` — Rust-only fixtures (out of scope)

## What done looks like

The bash-authored fixture consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; their consumers reference the generated names; the calibrant consts remain in the manifest; Rust builds deny-warnings clean and the fast suite is green.

51	  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
52	  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
53	  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
54	  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
55	  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
56	  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
57	  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
58	    "enrollment-validation"
59	    "pristine-lifecycle"
60	    "canonical-establish"
61	    "onboarding-sequence"
62	    "regime-validation"
63	    "regime-smoke"

65	    "hallmark-lifecycle"
66	    "tadmor"
67	    "moriah"
68	    "srjcl"
69	    "pluml"
70	  )
71	  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
72	  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
73	  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
74	  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
75	  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
76	  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
77	  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
78	  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
79	  # but creates no GCP project per run.
80	  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
81	    "enrollment-validation"
82	    "canonical-invest"
83	    "onboarding-sequence"
84	    "regime-validation"
85	    "regime-smoke"
86	    "dockerfile-hygiene"
87	    "tadmor"
88	    "moriah"
89	    "srjcl"
90	    "pluml"
91	  )
92	  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
93	  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
94	  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
95	  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
96	  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
97	  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
98	  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
99	  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
100	  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
101	    "canonical-invest"
102	    "dogfight"
103	  )
104	  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
105	  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
106	  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
107	  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
108	  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
109	  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
110	  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
111	  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
112	  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
113	    "kludge-tadmor"
114	    "tadmor"
115	  )

118	}

144

158	    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;

160	    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
161	    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;
162	    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish|dogfight|tadmor)" ;;

file.content:
# Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
    "canonical-invest"
    "dogfight"
  )
  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
    "kludge-tadmor"
    "tadmor"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

# zrbte_codegen() - Refresh all zipper-derived artifacts before compiling.
# Write-on-change, so unchanged files keep their mtime and cargo skips rebuilds.
# The build is the sole producer — there is no standalone generate command.
zrbte_codegen() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  rbz_generate_context "${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}" "${RBCC_tabtarget_context_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate tabtarget context"
  rbz_generate_consts "${RBCC_rbtdgc_consts_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate colophon consts"
}

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish|dogfight|tadmor)" ;;
  esac
}

# container name / compose service derives from these. Distinct from the
# credential RBCC_role_* family above (that names SA roles, a different axis —
# the names are not reused across the two to keep each word monosemous).
RBCC_container_bottle="bottle"
RBCC_container_pentacle="pentacle"
RBCC_container_sentry="sentry"

######################################################################
# Rust const projection (rbcc single-homed set → RBTDGC_)

# rbcc_emit_consts() - Emit the RBCC-owned co-maintained constants as Rust
# string consts to stdout, one `pub const` line per name/value pair via the
# shared buz_emit_const primitive (BUK must be kindled). The single-homed set:
# moorings/vessels dirs, credential roles, .env filenames. Each Rust const is
# RBTDGC_ + the RBCC stem (RBCC_ prefix stripped) uppercased; the value is
# carried verbatim. Bash stays mixed-case (RBCC_moorings_dir); the generated
# Rust is SCREAMING (RBTDGC_MOORINGS_DIR) per Rust convention — that casing is
# the sole transform, applied mechanically, so there is no per-entry mapping
# and no drift. rbtd's lib.rs paths, the manifest role mirror, and the
# rbtdrk/rbtdrp .env consts all source these instead of hand-copying.
rbcc_emit_consts() {
  printf '%s\n' "// RBCC constants (rbcc_Constants.sh single-homed set)"

  local z_name=""
  local z_stem=""
  local z_upper=""
  for z_name in \
    RBCC_moorings_dir    \
    RBCC_vessels_subdir  \
    RBCC_role_governor   \
    RBCC_role_retriever  \
    RBCC_role_director   \
    RBCC_role_payor      \
    RBCC_role_assay      \
    RBCC_role_mason      \
    RBCC_rbrr_file       \
    RBCC_rbrp_file       \
    RBCC_rbrm_file       \
    RBCC_rbrd_basename   \
    RBCC_rbrd_file       \
    RBCC_rbrs_file       \
    RBCC_rbrn_file       \
    RBCC_rbra_file       \
    RBCC_rbro_file       \
  ; do
    z_stem="${z_name#RBCC_}"
    z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_stem}" | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]')"
    buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${!z_name}" \
      || buc_die "rbcc_emit_consts: emit failed for ${z_name}"
  done
}

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbcc_*)

zrbcc_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc already kindled"

  # Curl timeout bounds — all actionable curl sites use these
  readonly RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC=10

zrbz_healthcheck() {
  zrbz_sentinel
  buz_healthcheck
}

######################################################################
# Rust const projection (colophons → RBTDGC_)

# rbz_emit_consts() - Emit the complete generated RBTDGC_ consts file to stdout.
# The single producer of rbtdgc_consts.rs content, shared by the theurge build
# (rbz_generate_consts) and rbq's freshness gate so the two never diverge.
# Two sections under one banner: the colophon group (this module binds the RBK
# projection prefixes — const name is RBTDGC_ + the enroll varname with its
# RBZ_ prefix stripped) followed by the rbcc single-homed set. rbcc_emit_consts
# is reachable here because every caller (rbq, the build, rblm) sources rbcc
# alongside rbz.
rbz_emit_consts() {
  zrbz_sentinel
  printf '%s\n' "// Generated by the theurge build — rbz colophons + rbcc constants. Do not edit."
  printf '%s\n' "// Regenerate: tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  buz_emit_colophon_consts "RBTDGC_" "RBZ_" || buc_die "rbz_emit_consts: colophon emit failed"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  rbcc_emit_consts || buc_die "rbz_emit_consts: rbcc emit failed"
}

# rbz_generate_consts() - Write the colophon const file, only on difference.
# Args: target_path
# Emits to a temp file, diffs against the target, and copies only when changed
# (stable timestamps). The single producer for the generated file — rblm and
# the theurge build wrapper both call this; rbq's gate verifies via rbz_emit_consts.
rbz_generate_consts() {
  zrbz_sentinel

  local -r z_target="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_target}" || buc_die "rbz_generate_consts: target path required"

  local -r z_tmp="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbz_generate_consts.rs"
  rbz_emit_consts > "${z_tmp}" || buc_die "rbz_generate_consts: emit failed"

  if test -f "${z_target}" && diff -q "${z_tmp}" "${z_target}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_log_args "Colophon consts already up to date: ${z_target}"
  else
    cp "${z_tmp}" "${z_target}" || buc_die "rbz_generate_consts: failed to write ${z_target}"
    buc_log_args "Generated: ${z_target}"
  fi
}

# rbz_generate_context() - Write the markdown tabtarget context, only on difference.
# Args: tt_dir, target_path
# Emits via buz_emit_context (which resolves frontispieces from tt_dir filenames),
# diffs against the target, and copies only when changed. Companion producer to
# rbz_generate_consts; the theurge build calls both, rbq's gate verifies via buz_emit_context.
rbz_generate_context() {
  zrbz_sentinel

  local -r z_tt_dir="${1:-}"
  local -r z_target="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_tt_dir}" || buc_die "rbz_generate_context: tabtarget dir required"
  test -n "${z_target}" || buc_die "rbz_generate_context: target path required"

file.content:
rbq_qualify_context() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying generated context freshness"

  local -r z_committed="${RBCC_tabtarget_context_file}"
  local -r z_fresh="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbq_context_check.md"

  buz_emit_context "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" > "${z_fresh}" || buc_die "Failed to generate context for comparison"

  if ! test -f "${z_committed}"; then
    buc_die "Generated context file missing: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  if ! diff -q "${z_fresh}" "${z_committed}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_die "Generated context file is stale: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  buc_log_args "Context file is fresh"
}

rbq_qualify_rust_consts() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying generated Rust consts freshness"

  local -r z_committed="${RBCC_rbtdgc_consts_file}"
  local -r z_fresh="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbq_rust_consts_check.rs"

  rbz_emit_consts > "${z_fresh}" || buc_die "Failed to generate Rust consts for comparison"

  if ! test -f "${z_committed}"; then
    buc_die "Generated Rust consts file missing: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  if ! diff -q "${z_fresh}" "${z_committed}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_die "Generated Rust consts file is stale: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  buc_log_args "Rust consts file is fresh"
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbq_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrn_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbq_Qualify.sh"

  zbuz_kindle
  zrbz_kindle
  zrbcc_kindle
  zrbq_kindle
}

buc_execute rbq_ "Recipe Bottle Qualification" zrbq_furnish "$@"

#!/bin/bash

# limitations under the License.

# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

content.text:
I've oriented and grounded the approach. Here's the mount of ₣BP → ₢BPAAG.

## Mounting ₢BPAAG (fixture-codegen-projection)

**Goal in one sentence:** Project the 18 bash-authored test-fixture names into generated `RBTDGC_*` consts (via the shared `buz_emit_const` primitive) and repoint the rbtd manifest's hand-mirror consumers onto them — leaving the 4 Rust-only calibrant fixtures hand-written.

**Heat context:** `rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup` (₣BP), 5/8 done. Theme: consolidate each co-maintained constant group to its single canonical bash owner and generate the Rust side. This pace mirrors the just-landed colophon (₢BPAAD) and rbcc (₢BPAAE) projections against the fixture-name group.

**Name assessment:** `fixture-codegen-projection` fits the docket exactly — no rename needed.

### What I found grounding the work
- The 18 bash-authored fixtures are the union of the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays in `rbte_engine.sh:42-115`. The 4 `CALIBRANT_*` fixtures have no bash home (Rust-only, `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`) — they stay in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. This matches the manifest's current 18+4 split exactly.
- `rbcc_emit_consts` (`rbcc_Constants.sh:120`) is the emit-shape template; `rbz_emit_consts` (`rbz_zipper.sh:246`) is the combiner.

### One real wrinkle — combiner wiring (needs a decision)
The rbcc projection's combiner wiring worked because **rbcc is sourced in every context that runs `rbz_emit_consts`**. Fixtures are different: `rbte_engine.sh` is sourced **only** by the theurge build (`rbte_cli.sh`). The other two callers of the combiner — the rbq freshness gate (`rbq_cli.sh:29`) and `rblm_cli.sh:388` — do **not** source it. Since all three must emit byte-identical output (build generates, the other two diff against it), the fixture data must be reachable in all three.

**Repair:** source `rbte_engine.sh` + `zrbte_kindle` into `rbq_cli.sh` and `rblm_cli.sh`, exactly mirroring how rbcc became universally available. `rbte_engine.sh` has a clean inclusion guard and a self-contained kindle, so this is low-risk. (Moving the fixture list into rbcc instead would violate the heat's locked "fixture home = rbte" decision, so I'm not proposing that.)

### Proposed approach
1. **Tidy** `rbte_engine.sh`: declare the 18 fixtures once as a flat `ZRBTE_FIXTURES` list; suite arrays stay literal subsets that select from it.
2. **Emit:** add `rbte_emit_consts` looping the flat list through `buz_emit_const` (`RBTDGC_` + stem, hyphens→underscores, uppercased), mirroring `rbcc_emit_consts`.
3. **Combine + wire:** call `rbte_emit_consts` from `rbz_emit_consts`; source+kindle rbte in `rbq_cli.sh` and `rblm_cli.sh`.
4. **Migrate consumers:** repoint the 18 `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consumers in the manifest onto the generated `RBTDGC_*`; keep the 4 calibrant consts and the runtime drift check.
5. **Verify:** `rbw-tb` (deny-warnings), `rbw-tt` (unit tests incl. manifest verify), `rbw-tq` (consts freshness gate), `rbw-ts` fast suite.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential, single agent — the bash and Rust edits are coupled (the freshness gate diffs generated against committed, so they must land together). Mechanical but cross-file; opus-level coordination, no parallelism gain.

Proceed with this approach, including the rbq/rblm sourcing repair?
Proceeding. Let me set up task tracking and read the files I'll edit.

14	//
15	// Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
16	//
17	// RBTDRM — colophon manifest verification for theurge
18	
19	// Colophon names are projected from the zipper registry into the generated
20	// RBTDGC_* consts (rbtdgc_consts.rs). This module consumes them for the
21	// per-fixture required-colophon manifest and the role→probe mapping; the
22	// runtime drift check (rbtdrm_verify) is retained until the build-time diff
23	// gate lands.
24	use crate::rbtdgc_consts::*;
25	
26	// Credential roles are projected from rbcc_Constants.sh into the generated
27	// RBTDGC_ROLE_* consts (rbtdgc_consts.rs) — consumed here and across the access
28	// probe surface. The former hand-written RBTDRM_ROLE_* mirror is retired.
29	
30	/// Map a credential role to its access-probe colophon. Returns None for
31	/// unknown roles. Replaces the former role-as-imprint scheme: each role now
32	/// names its own global tabtarget under the rbw-ac* family.
33	pub fn rbtdrm_credential_check_colophon(role: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
34	    match role {
35	        RBTDGC_ROLE_GOVERNOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_GOVERNOR),
36	        RBTDGC_ROLE_RETRIEVER => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER),
37	        RBTDGC_ROLE_DIRECTOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_DIRECTOR),
38	        RBTDGC_ROLE_PAYOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_PAYOR),
39	        _ => None,
40	    }
41	}
42	
43	// Fixture name consts — single definition per String Boundary Discipline.
44	// Crucible fixtures (charge/quench lifecycle)
45	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_TADMOR: &str = "tadmor";
46	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_MORIAH: &str = "moriah";
47	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_SRJCL: &str = "srjcl";
48	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_PLUML: &str = "pluml";
49	// Bare fixtures (GCP credentials, no container runtime)
50	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE: &str = "hallmark-lifecycle";
51	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_BATCH_VOUCH: &str = "batch-vouch";
52	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ACCESS_PROBE: &str = "access-probe";
53	// Fast fixtures (no external dependencies)
54	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION: &str = "enrollment-validation";
55	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_REGIME_VALIDATION: &str = "regime-validation";
56	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_REGIME_SMOKE: &str = "regime-smoke";
57	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HANDBOOK_RENDER: &str = "handbook-render";
58	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_DOCKERFILE_HYGIENE: &str = "dockerfile-hygiene";
59	// Pristine-lifecycle fixture (gate + SA/depot lifecycle cases)
60	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE: &str = "pristine-lifecycle";
61	// Gauntlet canonical-establish fixture (§2: canonical depot levy + governor
62	// mantle + retriever/director invest with per-case precondition probes)
63	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_ESTABLISH: &str = "canonical-establish";
64	// Skirmish canonical-invest fixture — the no-levy variant of
65	// canonical-establish. Reuses the three investiture cases (governor mantle +
66	// retriever/director invest) against a depot the operator has already levied
67	// by hand; omits depot-levy so the skirmish suite creates no GCP project per
68	// run. Distinction from canonical-establish is precondition, not behavior.
69	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST: &str = "canonical-invest";
70	// Gauntlet onboarding-sequence fixture (§3: handbook-walked vessel
71	// construction — inscribe reliquary, enshrine bases, kludge tadmor/ccyolo,
72	// plus one ordain-* case per director-mode handbook track, build-only)
73	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ONBOARDING_SEQUENCE: &str = "onboarding-sequence";
74	// Self-contained tadmor build fixture (rbw-ts.TestSuite.tadmor): kludges tadmor sentry+bottle
75	// locally and commits each hallmark (so the subsequent tadmor crucible fixture
76	// charges against a clean nameplate). Reuses onboarding's kludge helper minus
77	// its reliquary-stamp witness probe — local kludge has no GCP/reliquary dep.
78	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR: &str = "kludge-tadmor";
79	// Dogfight cloud-build viability fixture — standing-depot sibling to
80	// canonical-invest, proving the cloud-build → summon → run path yields a
81	// runnable artifact with NO crucible charged (the orthogonal axis skirmish
82	// covers). Ordains conjure-mode busybox, summons it, runs a degenerate
83	// container-runtime command proving executability, then abjures.
84	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_DOGFIGHT: &str = "dogfight";
85	// Calibrant fixtures — synthetic deterministic-verdict fixtures driving the
86	// bash blackbox testbench. Internal framework-test plumbing.
87	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_VERDICTS: &str = "calibrant-verdicts";
88	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_FAIL_FAST: &str = "calibrant-fail-fast";
89	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_PROGRESSING: &str = "calibrant-progressing";
90	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_SENTINEL: &str = "calibrant-sentinel";
91	
92	// Operation verb consts — single definition per String Boundary Discipline.
93	// Used for error-message prefixes, log labels, and commit messages where the
94	// operation name is a vocabulary unit. Atomic — compound operations like
95	// "kludge sentry" are composed at the call site from RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE
96	// and the relevant container-role constant.
97	pub const RBTDRM_OPERATION_INSCRIBE: &str = "inscribe";
98	pub const RBTDRM_OPERATION_YOKE: &str = "yoke";
99	pub const RBTDRM_OPERATION_ORDAIN: &str = "ordain";
100	pub const RBTDRM_OPERATION_ENSHRINE: &str = "enshrine";
101	pub const RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE: &str = "kludge";
102	
103	// Container role consts — sentry (security gateway) and bottle (workload) are
104	// the two container roles within a crucible. Used as suffixes for kludge
105	// operations and as commit-message vocabulary distinguishing the two builds.
106	pub const RBTDRM_CONTAINER_SENTRY: &str = "sentry";
107	pub const RBTDRM_CONTAINER_BOTTLE: &str = "bottle";
108	
109	// Regime-validation contract surfaces — the regime module theurge sources and
110	// the public *_probate entry it calls to drive a staged regime file through
111	// kindle+enforce. Single definition per String Boundary Discipline.
112	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRR: &str = "rbrr_regime.sh";
113	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRR: &str = "rbrr_probate";
114	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRD: &str = "rbrd_regime.sh";
115	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRD: &str = "rbrd_probate";
116	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRV: &str = "rbrv_regime.sh";
117	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRV: &str = "rbrv_probate";
118	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRN: &str = "rbrn_regime.sh";
119	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRN: &str = "rbrn_probate";
120	
121	/// Per-fixture required colophons. Returns None for unknown fixtures.
122	pub fn rbtdrm_required_colophons(fixture: &str) -> Option<&'static [&'static str]> {
123	    match fixture {
124	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_TADMOR
125	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_MORIAH
126	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_SRJCL
127	        | RBTDRM_FIXTURE_PLUML => Some(&[
128	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE,
129	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_QUENCH,
130	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_WRIT,
131	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_FIAT,
132	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_BARK,
133	            RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_ACTIVE,
134	        ]),
135	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE => Some(&[
136	            RBTDGC_ORDAIN_HALLMARK,
137	            RBTDGC_ABJURE_HALLMARK,
138	            RBTDGC_REKON_HALLMARK,

245	/// Verify that all required colophons for a fixture appear in the zipper manifest string.
246	pub fn rbtdrm_verify(manifest: &str, fixture: &str) -> Result<(), String> {
247	    let required = match rbtdrm_required_colophons(fixture) {
248	        Some(r) => r,
249	        None => {
250	            return Err(format!(
251	                "rbtd: unknown fixture '{}' — not registered in manifest",
252	                fixture
253	            ));
254	        }
255	    };
256	    for colophon in required {
257	        let found = manifest.split_whitespace().any(|token| token == *colophon);
258	        if !found {
259	            return Err(format!(
260	                "rbtd: colophon '{}' not found in zipper manifest (fixture '{}')",
261	                colophon, fixture
262	            ));
263	        }
264	    }
265	    Ok(())
266	}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrblm_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TEMP_DIR         " "Temporary directory for this invocation (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source rbcc_Constants.sh"

  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buym_yelp.sh"         || buc_die "Failed to source buym_yelp.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buh_handbook.sh"      || buc_die "Failed to source buh_handbook.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"     || buc_die "Failed to source buz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbz_zipper.sh"    || buc_die "Failed to source rbz_zipper.sh"
  zbuz_kindle
  zrbz_kindle
}

buc_execute rblm_ "Lifecycle Marshal" zrblm_furnish "$@"

# eof

1	<!--

3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# ❌ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:
85	
86	- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
87	- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable
88	
89	This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
90	
91	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
92	
93	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
94	
95	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
96	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
97	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
98	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
99	
100	### Regime Module Archetype
101	
102	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
103	
104	| Component | Location | Purpose |
105	|-----------|----------|---------|
106	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
107	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
108	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
109	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
110	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
111	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
112	
113	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
114	
115	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
116	
117	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Function Patterns
122	
123	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
124	
125	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
126	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
127	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
128	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
129	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
130	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
131	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
132	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
133	
134	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
135	
136	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
137	
138	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
139	
140	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
141	
142	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
143	
144	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
145	
146	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
147	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
148	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
149	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
150
