# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Rust const projection (fixture names → RBTDGC_)

# RBTE_fixtures — the canonical flat declaration of every bash-authored test
# fixture. Tinder constant (resolves at source time, no kindle): rbz_emit_consts
# reads it cross-module to project each name into the generated RBTDGC_ file. The
# ZRBTE_SUITE_* arrays in kindle stay literal subsets that select from this set —
# an unknown fixture name fails suite resolution, so declaration/suite drift is
# already caught. Calibrant fixtures are Rust-only synthetic (no bash home) and
# are absent here by design.
RBTE_fixtures=(
  "tadmor"
  "moriah"
  "srjcl"
  "pluml"
  "hallmark-lifecycle"
  "batch-vouch"
  "access-probe"
  "enrollment-validation"
  "regime-validation"
  "regime-smoke"
  "handbook-render"
  "dockerfile-hygiene"
  "pristine-lifecycle"
  "canonical-establish"
  "canonical-invest"
  "onboarding-sequence"
  "kludge-tadmor"
  "dogfight"
)

# rbte_emit_consts() - Emit the bash-authored fixture names as Rust string consts
# to stdout via the shared buz_emit_const primitive (BUK must be kindled). Each
# Rust const is RBTDGC_ + the fixture name uppercased with hyphens mapped to
# underscores; the value is the fixture name verbatim. Mirrors rbcc_emit_consts.
rbte_emit_consts() {
  printf '%s\n' "// Fixture names (rbte_engine.sh suite set)"

  local z_fixture=""
  local z_upper=""
  for z_fixture in "${RBTE_fixtures[@]}"; do
    z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_fixture}" | tr '[:lower:]-' '[:upper:]_')"
    buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${z_fixture}" \
      || buc_die "rbte_emit_consts: emit failed for ${z_fixture}"
  done
}

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
    "canonical-invest"
    "dogfight"
  )
  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
    "kludge-tadmor"
    "tadmor"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

# zrbte_codegen() - Refresh all zipper-derived artifacts before compiling.
# Write-on-change, so unchanged files keep their mtime and cargo skips rebuilds.
# The build is the sole producer — there is no standalone generate command.
zrbte_codegen() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  rbz_generate_context "${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}" "${RBCC_tabtarget_context_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate tabtarget context"
  rbz_generate_consts "${RBCC_rbtdgc_consts_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate colophon consts"
}

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish|dogfight|tadmor)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture — pass one as the folio (e.g. rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh tadmor)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  local z_case="${1:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

# eof

-# Rust const is RBTDGC_ + the fixture name uppercased with hyphens mapped to
-# underscores; the value is the fixture name verbatim. Mirrors rbcc_emit_consts.

content.text:
Tree clean, tasks cleared. Now the reslate.

## The reslate question

The pace was built on "bash authors fixture names → project them to Rust." Reality is the reverse: Rust authors them (`RBTDRC_FIXTURES`, fixture structs naming themselves via `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`). So the real question isn't "how do we project fixtures" — it's **"is there a fixture-name redundancy worth removing at all, and if so, in which direction?"**

The only duplication that actually exists: the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays in `rbte_engine.sh` spell fixture names as raw string literals, duplicating the Rust-owned names. Three ways to read that:

**Option 1 — Drop fixtures from this heat (record the premise correction).**
A suite *is* a human-curated selection ("the fast suite is these five") — you wouldn't auto-generate its membership; that's a design choice, not a co-maintained constant. And `main.rs:200` already rejects unknown fixture names at runtime, so drift is caught. The bash literals aren't the kind of mechanically-co-maintained constant this heat targets. Cleanest MVP answer: drop the pace, capture a scar so it isn't re-attempted.

**Option 2 — Consolidate suites into Rust (rightful-owner relocation).**
Move suite definitions next to the fixtures that own the names. Bash's `rbw-ts` would forward a *suite* name to the binary exactly as `rbw-tf` forwards a *fixture* name — and the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` literals disappear entirely. This is the truest match to the heat's "consolidate to the single rightful owner" theme, and needs **no codegen**. But it's the largest change (suite resolution moves bash→Rust).

**Option 3 — Rust→bash codegen.**
Keep suites in bash, but generate the fixture-name vocabulary from `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` so a bad name is a build error instead of a runtime error. This invents a new codegen direction (Rust→bash) the heat never built, and inverts the build ordering (bash artifact would depend on the built binary). I'd avoid it — most machinery for least gain.

**My lean:** Option 1. The fixture-name "redundancy" is weak — suite membership is inherently curated, and runtime validation already guards it. There's no co-maintained-constant problem here of the sort the rest of the heat fixes. If you feel the bash literals genuinely itch, Option 2 is the principled fix, but it's a real refactor that deserves its own pace and probably its own heat-shape note (it's about test-vocabulary ownership, not constant projection).

Which direction sits right with you? And if it's Option 1, do you want the premise correction recorded as a scar, a paddock note, or just the reslated docket body?

content: Mounting pace ₢BPAAG in heat ₣BP, we found its premise inverted (Rust authors fixture names, not bash), so I reverted my three bash edits and the tree is clean. Next: you pick the reslate direction (my lean is dropping fixtures from this heat). (disable recaps in /config)

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  local z_case="${1:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

lastPrompt: Well the idea of pulling suites fully into theurge does reduce brain friction.  Lets discuss this more as the replacement for this pace?

content.text:
Everything checks out for this to be clean. `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2358) is already a static array of fixture refs with a `rbtdrc_lookup_fixture` by-name finder; `zrbte_resolve_suite` has exactly one caller; the 8 suite tabtargets just carry the suite name as an imprint. So the binary already owns everything except the *groupings*.

## The shape

Today bash knows three test axes and Rust knows two:

| Axis | Tabtarget | Today | After |
|------|-----------|-------|-------|
| One case | `rbw-tc` | bash forwards `single <fixture> <case>` | unchanged |
| One fixture | `rbw-tf` | bash forwards `<fixture>` | unchanged |
| A suite | `rbw-ts` | **bash resolves the group, loops, calls binary per fixture** | bash forwards `suite <name>` |

The win is symmetry: bash becomes a pure forwarder on all three axes, and "what fixtures exist + how they group" lives in one place (theurge). The `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays and `zrbte_resolve_suite` get deleted; `rbte_suite` collapses to build-binary + forward.

**Why it's low-friction to build:** the bash loop (`rbte_engine.sh:214`) is genuinely thin — `buc_step`, invoke binary, repeat. There's no per-fixture bash orchestration (charge/quench is already inside each fixture's Rust setup/teardown). So nothing crucible-related has to move; only the iteration and the group lists.

## The decisions I want your read on

**1. How suites name their members.** Two ways:
- **Fixture refs** (`&[&rbtdre_Fixture]`, pointing at the same statics `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` holds) — membership becomes *compile-checked*. A typo or a deleted fixture won't compile. Strictly better than today's runtime check.
- **Name strings** — a literal 1:1 move of the bash arrays into Rust, validated at runtime against `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` (same safety as today, just relocated). Simpler, lower-risk diff.

I lean refs — it's the bigger correctness gain and the reason to do this at all. But it surfaces a sub-question ↓

**2. Composition vs. enumeration.** Bash gets DRY for free: `SERVICE`/`CRUCIBLE`/`COMPLETE` are all `FAST + extras`, so editing `FAST` updates all of them. Rust const slices don't concatenate cleanly. To keep `FAST` single-source I'd make the derived suites small functions returning a composed `Vec` (negligible cost); full enumeration is simpler but reintroduces a 4-place edit when `FAST` changes. Which do you prefer — preserve composition, or flat explicit lists?

**3. Reporting moves to Rust.** The suite-level progress and "N fixtures complete" summary currently come from bash (`buc_step`/`buc_success`). In Rust you'd get one unified cross-fixture summary instead. Likely an improvement, but it's a console-UX change you've been looking at for a while — OK to change it?

**4. The operator-precondition comments.** The skirmish/dogfight/gauntlet/tadmor arrays carry real operator knowledge ("PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied"). Those move into the Rust suite defs as doc comments — agreed that's where they belong?

**Mint:** the suite defs want a home. `rbtdrs_suites.rs` (prefix `rbtdrs`, "s" for suites) is free in the `rbtdr*` impl family and terminal-clean. Good, or would you rather they sit beside the registry in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`?

**Risk to flag:** this is the spine of every `rbw-ts` run, so it's load-bearing test infra — verification is "run `rbw-ts fast` and confirm the same fixtures execute in the same order," not just a green build.

My instinct on the bundle: refs + preserved composition + Rust reporting + new `rbtdrs` module. Where do you come down, especially on 1 and 2?

content.text:
Good — your answers mostly land, and the two you flagged are exactly the ones worth slowing on. Let me grounded-check each against what I just read, and surface what's left.

## 1) Fixture refs + "macro magic for names"

Refs: yes, unreserved. Each fixture is already a named `pub static` (`RBTDRF_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION`, etc.) and `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` already lists them by `&`-ref (rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2358). A suite becomes the same shape — `&[&'static rbtdre_Fixture]` pointing at those statics. Reference a renamed/deleted fixture → it won't compile. That's the upgrade over today's runtime check.

The macro part needs a distinction I should have drawn earlier — **two different "names" are in play:**

- **Suite names** — `fast`, `service`, `crucible`, … — all single lowercase words. These are a *clean* fit for your idea: a macro derives the wire-name from the identifier via `stringify!` (the `case!` macro at rbtdre_engine.rs:137 already does exactly this for case names). So the macro magic lands naturally at the suite level.
- **Fixture names** — `enrollment-validation`, `kludge-tadmor`, … — kebab-case, and they double as tabtarget imprints (`rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh`), operator-typed args (`rbw-tf access-probe`), and doc vocabulary. `stringify!(RBTDRF_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION)` gives the wrong string, and converting it (strip prefix, lowercase, `_`→`-`) needs a const-fn transform **and** retiring the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consts, which ripples to ~13 consumer files.

So my concern: the macro is great for **suite** names but fights the **fixture** name shape. My recommendation — apply the macro to suites, leave fixture names exactly as they are (the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consts stay, untouched). Deriving fixture names from identifiers is a separate, bigger question (it's a fixture-identity change, not a suite change) and can be its own pace if you ever want it. Does keeping fixtures out of scope sit right, or were you specifically after killing the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings?

## 2) Composition vs. enumeration — let me redo this

Here's the concrete thing. In bash today:

```bash
ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=( enrollment-validation regime-validation regime-smoke handbook-render dockerfile-hygiene )
ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=( "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}"  access-probe hallmark-lifecycle batch-vouch )
ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=( "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" tadmor srjcl pluml )
```

`SERVICE` and `CRUCIBLE` *include* `FAST` by reference. Add a fixture to `FAST` and all three grow automatically — **one edit.** That's "composition."

The **4-place edit** only happens if we go the other way ("enumeration"): re-list FAST's five fixtures verbatim inside SERVICE, CRUCIBLE, and COMPLETE. Then adding one fast fixture means editing four lists by hand, which drift apart the moment someone forgets one. That's the trap you're rejecting.

So your instinct is decisive and I agree: **preserve composition.** Mechanism in Rust: define `FAST` once as a base ref-slice, and have the derived suites compose it (`[fast, extras].concat()` in the resolver). It's resolved once per run, so the tiny runtime cost is irrelevant, and there are zero new dependencies. One edit to FAST still propagates everywhere. No 4-place edits.

## 3) Reporting → Rust

Confirmed, taking it. One rider: I'll make sure Rust still prints a per-fixture header as it goes (it already prints per *case*), so a 12-fixture gauntlet still shows live progress rather than going quiet.

## 4) The precondition strings — you've improved my idea

You read it better than I pitched it. My version was "don't lose the comment prose." Yours is "**print it at runtime so the context is visible when it fails**" — which is strictly better and is literally what BCG says to do (runtime visibility over comments). So: each suite carries a human `description` + optional `precondition` field, and the suite header prints them before running. `rbw-ts skirmish` would announce "PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — run rbw-dL by hand first" up top, so a failure has its context attached. That turns those buried bash comments into operator-facing output. Folding it in.

(This means a suite isn't a bare `&[&Fixture]` but a small struct — name, description, precondition, members — which is also the natural thing for the `suite!` macro to build.)

## Remaining concerns

- **Calibrant fixtures sit in no suite.** They're in `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` but absent from every `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`; they run via `rbw-tf`/unit tests. After the move they stay suite-less — not orphaned, just not grouped. Flagging so it's not mistaken for a regression.
- **Macro implementation has one fiddly bit.** Deriving both the wire-name string *and* a static's identifier from one macro arg normally wants the `paste` crate, which this codebase refuses (zero-dep). Solvable with a single registry-style macro that emits the resolver directly instead of per-suite statics — but I'm calling it now as a mount-time implementation detail, not a blocker.
- **This is the spine of every `rbw-ts` run.** Verification isn't "it builds" — it's "run `rbw-ts fast`, confirm the same fixtures execute in the same order," and spot-check that each suite name resolves to the identical membership it has today. I'll diff old-vs-new membership explicitly.
- **No build-ordering hazard** (unlike the Rust→bash codegen option we discarded): bash still builds the binary, then forwards `suite <name>`. Clean.

Net scope for the reslated pace: suite defs move into a new `rbtdrs_suites.rs` (refs + `suite!` macro + description/precondition), Rust gains a `suite` dispatch mode, `rbte_suite`/`zrbte_resolve_suite`/`ZRBTE_SUITE_*` collapse to a one-line forwarder, fixture names untouched. Does that boundary match what you're picturing?

content.text:
Confirmed how calibrants run — that changes my answer on them. Here's where I land on each.

## 1) I think we're actually aligned — with one real fork left

Re-reading you, the principle is: **Rust identifiers are the canonical identity; don't maintain a parallel kebab-string namespace to guess at.** Bash works at suite granularity (a small set you run regularly) and never needs fixture/case names surfaced to it. I'm with you on all of that.

And the suite pace *already* embodies it: suite membership is fixture **refs** (`&RBTDRF_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION` — fully-minted Rust names, no strings), and the suite's own name derives from its identifier via the macro. So nothing in the suite work needs a kebab string.

Where I think we *might still* differ — and the thing I want a clean yes/no on — is **the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab strings themselves** (`"enrollment-validation"`, etc.). Those are the `.name` field on each fixture and the wire-name an operator types for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc`. Your philosophy says kill them (let the Rust identifier be the name). I agree in principle. My only hesitation is **scope coupling**: retiring them changes what you type for individual runs, and touches ~13 consumer files plus the manifest-verify logic — on top of the suite spine. Two clean ways:

- **(a) This pace = suites only.** Fixtures keep `.name = RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` untouched. Tight, verifiable (the suite spine is risky enough on its own). Retiring the kebab strings becomes the *next* pace.
- **(b) One sweep.** Suites + retire `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` + identifier-derive fixture names, all here.

I lean **(a)** purely for verification hygiene, not principle. Your call — that's the last open design question.

## Calibrants — not an omission, a different execution mode

I checked: calibrants *do* run regularly — under **`rbw-tt`** (cargo test), via `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. That module drives each calibrant fixture through the real dispatch and **asserts expected verdicts, including expected *failures*** (`rbtdtl_assert_fail_with` at line 68 — a calibrant case that's *supposed* to fail is asserted to fail). That's the whole point of a calibrant: it proves the harness correctly detects pass/fail/skip/sentinel.

That's exactly why they can't drop into an `rbw-ts` suite: their intentional Fail/Skip cases would count as red suite failures. The expected-verdict knowledge lives in the `rbtdtl` test module, and duplicating it into the suite runner would be redundant.

So the real shape of your concern is **cadence, not membership**: are you running `rbw-tt` as faithfully as `rbw-ts`? If "yes," calibrants are covered. If the itch is "I want one command that's genuinely everything," that's a small orchestration question (a wrapper that runs `rbw-tt` + `rbw-ts complete`) — and I'd treat it as its own itch, not bend the suite model. Does that reframing settle it, or do you want that unifying "truly complete" entry point?

## Macro — native, zero new crates, spelled in the docket

Firmly agreed, and noted as a standing constraint. The native shape needs no `paste` and no crate: a single `macro_rules!` that emits the resolver directly, deriving each suite's wire-name from its identifier via `stringify!`, and expressing composition as `base-suite + extras` concatenated at resolve time (so `FAST` stays single-source, no 4-place edits). I'll write that into the docket as a locked constraint with a concrete sketch, so the mount agent doesn't re-derive it or reach for a crate.

---

We're converging. Two answers left and I can write the reslate: **(1a) suites-only vs (1b) one sweep**, and **whether the calibrant concern is cadence (settled) or you want a "truly complete" entry point**.

content.text:
## Is "calibrant" a term of art?

Yes — borrowed from metrology/analytical chemistry, where a *calibrant* is a reference material with a **known, certified value** you feed an instrument to confirm it measures correctly. Applied here as a metaphor: a fixture with a **known, deterministic verdict** (pass/fail/skip/sentinel) fed through the test engine to confirm the *harness itself* reports outcomes correctly — including correctly reporting a known-fail as a fail. It's an apt term, but it lives only in code (`rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`, the manifest comment) and appears in **no** CLAUDE.md or rbk context file — which is exactly why you hadn't met it. Your instinct to surface it is right.

## Calibrants: hold separate + document — agreed, folding it into scope

That resolves the fork cleanly: calibrants stay out of `rbw-ts` suites (their intentional failures would red a suite), keep running under `rbw-tt`, and get **documented** so they're discoverable instead of mysterious. And your broader point — *the work should leave the navigational context correct so a restarted chat navigates the new test system without rediscovering it* — becomes an explicit done-criterion of the pace. Likely two touch points: the root **CLAUDE.md "Test Execution"** table (suites now resolved in theurge; the `rbw-tt` vs `rbw-ts` split) and **`Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md`** (what calibrants are, why they're held separate). This is genuinely part of the change, not an afterthought.

## Where this started (you resumed from yesterday)

You said "mount BP." We mounted heat **₣BP** (rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup), next pace **₢BPAAG** (fixture-codegen-projection). Its docket said "project the *bash-authored* fixture names into generated Rust consts." I began implementing, you paused me over a comment, and we discovered the docket's premise was **inverted** — Rust authors fixtures, bash only has redundant copies in its suite arrays. I reverted the wrong-direction edits (tree is clean). We then redesigned the pace into: **pull test suites into theurge** so bash becomes a pure forwarder and the test vocabulary lives in one place.

## The context-rich action: capture the design in the docket *now*

The implementation can happen later (likely a fresh chat) — but **only if the design survives this chat ending.** The durable carrier is the docket. So the action to take while context is rich is to **reslate ₢BPAAG**, plus a brief **paddock note** so the premise-correction isn't re-attempted by a future pace.

Concretely, two writes:

1. **Reslate ₢BPAAG** — new shape (suites→theurge), with locked constraints: suite membership as compile-checked fixture refs; suite names via a **native `macro_rules!`, zero new crates**, deriving names through `stringify!`; composition as base-suite + extras concatenated at resolve time (single-source, no 4-place edits); runtime-printed suite description/precondition; bash `rbte_suite`/`zrbte_resolve_suite`/`ZRBTE_SUITE_*` collapse to a forwarder; **navigational context updated**; calibrants held separate and documented. Done = `rbw-ts fast` runs the identical fixture set in identical order, build clean, context files current.
2. **Paddock note on ₣BP** — record that the fixture-name group is Rust-authored, so it's *out* of the bash→Rust projection; its redundancy is addressed by relocating suites into theurge instead. (Shape-level, so future paces don't re-try the projection.)

## The one decision still open

Scope of the kebab fixture strings (`RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*`):
- **(a) suites-only** — leave fixture `.name` consts untouched; retiring them is a *later* pace. Tighter, the spine-of-`rbw-ts` change verifies on its own.
- **(b) one sweep** — also retire `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` and identifier-derive fixture names here (touches ~13 files + manifest-verify + the `rbw-tf` wire-names you type).

I recommend **(a)**. Give me the (a)/(b) call and I'll write the reslate + paddock note right now while it's all loaded.

# jjezs_reslate BPAAG

## Character

Intricate test-infrastructure refactor carrying a premise correction: the prior docket had ownership backwards. Rust authors fixtures (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry, fixture structs naming themselves); bash only holds redundant copies in its suite arrays. This is not a bash→Rust projection — it is a consolidation of test vocabulary into its rightful owner. Touches the spine of every `rbw-ts` run, so verification is behavioral, not just a green build.

## Goal

Make theurge (Rust) the sole owner of test vocabulary: relocate suite definitions from bash into theurge, and collapse fixture-name identity onto Rust identifiers — retiring every hand-maintained name string on both sides of the boundary.

## Shape

One end state ("Rust owns what fixtures exist, how they group, and what they're named"), reached along two strands:

- **Suites into theurge.** The `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays and `zrbte_resolve_suite` in `rbte_engine.sh` are the redundant copy of Rust-owned fixture names. Move suite definitions into a new Rust module (minted prefix `rbtdrs`, "suites"). A suite is compile-checked **fixture refs** (the statics `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` already holds), never name strings. `rbte_suite` collapses to: build binary, forward the suite name; the binary grows a `suite` dispatch mode beside its existing `single` mode. Each suite carries a human description + optional precondition **printed at runtime** at suite start, so a failure shows its context — superseding the buried bash precondition comments.

- **Fixture identity = Rust identifier.** Retire the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab consts; a fixture's name derives from its Rust identifier (the `case!` macro in `rbtdre_engine.rs` is the stringify precedent). Repoint every consumer (`grep RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`) and `rbtdrm_required_colophons` onto the derived names. Operator wire-names for `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc` shift kebab→identifier; accepted, since individual fixture runs are rare.

## Locked constraints

- **Zero new crates.** The suite registry and name derivation use native `macro_rules!` only — no `paste`, no concat crate. Derive names via `stringify!`; express composition as base-suite + extras concatenated at resolve time, so a base like `fast` stays single-source (no flat re-enumeration, no multi-place edits).
- **Calibrants stay out of `rbw-ts` suites.** Their cases intentionally Fail/Skip to calibrate the harness, asserted under `rbw-tt` (`rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`). They are documented, not suited.
- **Navigational context is part of done.** Update the CLAUDE.md "Test Execution" section and `Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-theurge-ifrit-context.md` so a restarted chat navigates the new model without rediscovery — including a definition of "calibrant" and the `rbw-tt` vs `rbw-ts` split.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — suite arrays + `rbte_suite` (→ forwarder)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry + lookup
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdre_engine.rs` — `Fixture` struct + `case!` stringify precedent
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` — binary dispatch (add `suite` mode)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` — calibrant meta-tests (held separate)

## What done looks like

`rbw-ts fast` runs the identical fixture set in identical order as today, and every suite name resolves to the membership it has now. No `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`/`zrbte_resolve_suite` in bash; no `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings anywhere. `rbw-tb` builds deny-warnings clean, `rbw-tt` green (calibrants included), `rbw-tq` clean. CLAUDE.md and the theurge context file describe the new test model and define "calibrant."

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — suite arrays + `rbte_suite` (→ forwarder)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` — `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry + lookup
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdre_engine.rs` — `Fixture` struct + `case!` stringify precedent
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs` — binary dispatch (add `suite` mode)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` hand-mirror + `rbtdrm_required_colophons`
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` — calibrant meta-tests (held separate)

`rbw-ts fast` runs the identical fixture set in identical order as today, and every suite name resolves to the membership it has now. No `ZRBTE_SUITE_*`/`zrbte_resolve_suite` in bash; no `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` strings anywhere. `rbw-tb` builds deny-warnings clean, `rbw-tt` green (calibrants included), `rbw-tq` clean. 1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BP
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.
6	
7	The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.
8	
9	## Locked decisions
10	
11	- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
12	- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
13	- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
14	- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.
15	
16	## What done looks like
17	
18	The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).
19

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

# jjezs_paddock BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All four co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Co-maintained constant set in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earn a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.
- **Fixture names are Rust-authored — out of the projection.** On inspection, fixtures are defined in Rust (the `RBTDRC_FIXTURES` registry; fixture structs name themselves), and bash holds only redundant copies in its `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` suite arrays — there is no canonical bash author, so fixtures fail the single-author rule. The redundancy is removed in the other direction: suite definitions relocate into theurge (Rust) and bash becomes a pure forwarder. This is an ownership relocation, not a bash→Rust constant projection — a future pace must not re-attempt projecting fixture names.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

content.text:
Done — both durable captures are committed and the tree is clean.

## What landed

**Reslated ₢BPAAG** (commit `11b76c4d1`) — the docket is now the suites-into-theurge consolidation, full sweep:
- Suites relocate into a new Rust module (`rbtdrs`), membership as compile-checked fixture refs, bash collapses to a forwarder, runtime-printed preconditions.
- Fixture identity becomes the Rust identifier; the `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` kebab strings retire.
- Locked: zero new crates (native `macro_rules!`, `stringify!`-derived names, base+extra composition), calibrants held out of `rbw-ts` and documented, navigational-context updates are part of "done."
- The Character line records the premise correction so a fresh mount agent won't re-invert it.

**Updated paddock ₣BP** (commit `05c64a5a8`) — removed "fixture names" from the bash→Rust projection groups (now four, not five) and added a locked decision stating fixtures are Rust-authored, out of the projection, addressed by relocating suites — explicitly warning future paces not to re-attempt projecting fixture names.

## Where this leaves you

The design from this whole conversation is now durable in the docket + paddock, so the implementation can happen in a fresh chat without losing context. Nothing is half-applied — the three exploratory bash edits were reverted earlier, and the only changes on disk are the two jjx commits.

When you're ready to build it, "mount BP" in a new session will surface the reslated docket. One thing I'd flag for that future mount: this is a sizable pace (suite spine + the ~13-file fixture-name migration + context docs) — if at mount it feels like it wants splitting, that's your call to make then. Thanks for the close attention on the premise; catching it saved a wrong-direction landing.
